content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO 95804 • • • 100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 9581
Technical Analyst and Hearings ... Executive Legal ... Enforcement ... Conflict of Interest
9 1 6 - 3 2 7 - 5 6 0 0 916-327-17 9 1 6 - 3 2 7 - 5 6 0 0 9 1 6 - 3 2 7 - 5 6 1 4

September 15, 1977

A-77-09-022

Honorable Kenneth Cory
State Controller
P.O. Box 1019
Sacramento, CA 95805

Dear Mr. Cory:

During conversations with Michael Baker of our staff, you have presented the following situation for review by the Commission. Starting in late 1974 or early 1975 and extending through part of 1976, you were the subject of investigations which were conducted by the Orange County Grand Jury, the Los Angeles U.S. Attorney's office and a Federal grand jury in Sacramento. The Orange County and Los Angeles investigations principally concerned alleged wrongdoing by Dr. Louis Cella, and your relationship with Dr. Cella was looked into as part of that investigation. The Sacramento investigation concerned allegations that you had taken bribes to appoint persons to the post of inheritance tax referee. In addition, in 1972 a lawsuit was filed against you and others by William Dannemeyer, your opponent in the 1972 Assembly race, in which it was alleged that you conspired to libel him during the campaign.

The law firm of Broad, Stourie and Schulz has represented you in these matters. In August 1974, you paid the firm $500. It was then agreed that you would make no further payments to the firm until the cases were concluded. You indicate that this was in accord with your law firm's customary practice of not demanding payment of fees from many of its ongoing clients until the completion of a case.

You also indicate that when these proceedings commenced, you decided that if you were ultimately cleared of the various charges, your campaign committee would pay the legal fees incurred. If you were not exonerated, on the other hand, it was your decision to pay the legal fees out of personal funds. As far as your law firm was concerned, however, the debt was your personal responsibility. In other words, although your law firm would have accepted payment from your committee since it was no particular con-

Honorable Kenneth Cory
September 15, 1977
Page Two


cern to the firm who paid the fees so long as they were
paid, it was understood that if you were cleared but your
committee had insufficient funds to pay the fees, you would
remain individually liable for the debt.

During 1976, all these matters were concluded in
your favor. The total legal fees due to Broad, Khourie and
Schulz amounted to $12,674.56. Your campaign committee had
insufficient funds in 1976 to pay the fees, and therefore,
no decision was made at that time as to whether you or your
committee would pay the law firm. Your committee was successful in raising funds this year, however, and is now prepared
to pay the fees due Broad, Khourie and Schulz. You ask how
these payments should be reported.

Your campaign committee should report these payments
as expenditures on the semi-annual statement for the period
in which the payments are made. No amendment of past statements is required. If prior to this year the decision had
been made, or it was at least understood, that your campaign
committee would pay the legal fees, then the committee would
have been required to disclose its share of the debt at the
time that decision was made.1/ But it is our understanding
that this was not the case and that you were ultimately
responsible for the debt, not your committee. Your committee
will incur a reportable expense, therefore, only when it
decides voluntarily to pay the bill.

Your inquiry raises two other questions. First,
even though your committee had no obligation to disclose
these accrued expenses when they were incurred in 1975 and
1976, should you have disclosed the debt as an accrued expense in 1975 and 1976 on your candidate's statement. The
answer is no. If you had raised money in those years for
the express purpose of paying your legal fees, we would
regard the situation differently. In such a case, the money


1/ This is because Government Code Section 82025
provides that a campaign expenditure is deemed to have been
made, and therefore is reportable, "on the date the payment
is made or on the date consideration, if any, is received,
whichever is earlier." (Emphasis added.) We would consider
the committee to have received consideration on the date it
is decided that it was responsible for payment.

"Honorable Kenneth Cory
September 15, 1977
Page Three

raised and the legal fees incurred would have had to be
reported on a campaign statement since the legal problems to
which they relate are basically political in nature and
concern your status as an officeholder and as a possible
future candidate. Since you advise us that you were not
raising money in 1975 and 1976 for this earmarked purpose,
however, you were not required to disclose the accrued fees
on a campaign statement at that time.

The second question is whether payment of your
legal fees by your committee constitutes a gift to you which
must be disclosed on your Statement of Economic Interests.
Again, the answer is no. Payments by a campaign committee
which provide a purely personal benefit to an officeholder,
such as the purchase of furniture for an officeholder's
home, would have to be reported by the official on his State-
ment of Economic Interests as a gift from the committee.
But payments by a committee of an officeholder's debts which
are primarily political in nature do not constitute a gift
to the official. The legal fees in question here fall into
that category.

If you have any questions please contact me or
Michael Baker, the Director of the Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:MJB:plh"
